unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 8! This unit is all about the tools, machines, and everyday items we use at home, at school, and at work. You will learn the names of many useful objects — from a stapler and a microwave to a telescope and a helicopter. You will also learn how to describe what these things do, how to give safe instructions for using them, and how to talk about working together as a crew. By the end of the unit, you will be ready to read and enjoy this unit's mystery story, “The Attic Clue”, about a class in Rwanda who discover old tools in a dusty attic and build something amazing together.
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few words each day.
Work through the seven mini-lessons in the Grammar file.
Read the Story file, “The Attic Clue”, and answer the questions.
Work through the four skills below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see what you have learned.

visual.alt: Learners investigating tools, machines and useful everyday items

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name and describe many tools, machines, and everyday items in English.
Describe an object using adjectives and a relative clause with who, that, or which.
Say what people have to do to stay safe when using tools and machines.
Make promises about the future using will.
Tell a short story about the past using the past simple tense.
Give clear instructions using imperatives, like “Use”, “Put”, and “Clean”.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Explain reasons using because and so that.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

readings.title: The Right Tool for the Job
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: Every job needs the right tool. When Sophia wants to join her homework pages together, she uses a stapler. When her mother wants to heat the soup quickly, she uses the microwave. When Dad carries his work papers to the office, he puts them in a briefcase. Big jobs need big machines. In a factory, heavy machinery helps people build cars and lift boxes. On the farm, a strong crew works together with their tools. The right tool, used the right way, makes hard work easy — and it keeps us safe too.

readings.title: A Look at the Stars
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: On a clear night in the desert, the sky is full of stars. Long ago, people could only look up with their eyes. Then someone built a telescope — a tool that helps us see things that are very far away. With a telescope, you can see the craters on the moon and the rings around a planet. A telescope is a machine that brings the sky closer. It shows us that the world is bigger and more wonderful than we thought.

readings.title: How to Use the Microwave Safely
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: “Hello! Today I will show you how to use a microwave safely. First, put your food in a bowl that is safe for the microwave. Never use metal, because metal can cause sparks. Next, close the door and set the time. You have to stay close so that the food does not get too hot. When it beeps, open the door carefully — the bowl will be hot! Use a cloth to take it out. Stir the food with a utensil before you eat. Enjoy your warm meal!”

readings.title: The Helper Vehicles Song
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: “Up goes the helicopter, spinning in the sky. / Fast comes the ambulance, hurry, hurry by! / Big machines to help us, working night and day. / Thank you to the people who keep us safe this way!”

readings.title: The Attic Clue
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
High in the green hills of Rwanda, a small village school sat beside tall trees and rolling farms. It had three classrooms, a dusty library, and a small attic just under the roof.
One morning, the teacher came in carrying a big box. “This week,” she said, “we will work together as a crew. We will clean and explore the attic. Then you will write a story about what you find.”
The students gasped. The attic had been locked for years. What secrets could be up there?
“I will open it tomorrow,” said the teacher. “But we have to wear safety equipment — gloves and masks — because the attic may be dusty.”
The next day, the students put on gloves and cloth masks. They took flashlights, brooms, and a folder to hold any papers they found.
Hannah climbed the ladder first. “It is dark,” she said, “but it is not scary.”
Her friend Ishmael followed close behind. “Look — there is a big curtain covering something!”
They pulled the curtain away and found an old wooden table covered with tools: a stapler, some broken pens, and even a small telescope.
“Look at this!” said Hannah. She pointed to a briefcase with stickers of aeroplanes and stars.
They opened it and found drawings of machines and a note that said: “For the student who will build the future.”
Downstairs, the teacher smiled. “This school was started long ago by a woman who loved science. Perhaps she left this for someone special.”
“We want to build something!” said Ishmael.
“Then you must plan like a real team,” said the teacher. “Choose your tools and your resources, and work together.”
The students picked their favourite items. “We will use the machinery from the old art room,” said Ishmael. “It still works.”
“I will clean all the parts,” said Hannah, “and we will write an article about it.”
“You have to be careful with sharp things,” warned the teacher. “Use the hardware safely.”
“We will,” the whole class promised.
That night, Hannah wrote in her notebook: “We found a dusty attic and old tools. We have to clean everything. Tomorrow, we will build something useful.”
The next morning, the crew met early. They carried the parts in and cleaned the work area. Ishmael warmed some cocoa in the microwave to share with everyone.
“Do not forget to bring a utensil,” said Hannah. “We cannot stir it with our fingers!” She handed out spoons, and everyone laughed.
Little by little, they built a small toy helicopter. It had buttons and even a tiny shield made of plastic. “This shield is for safety!” said Ishmael.
They tested it outside. It lifted off, spun in the air, and landed gently on the grass.
A young boy clapped his hands. “It flies!”
“Let us write a poster,” said Hannah. “We will describe what it does and how we built it.” Together they wrote:
“This is a flying machine that uses clean energy. It has a shield that protects its wires. It was built by a crew who worked hard together.”
At the school fair, parents and children came to see the project. One mother said, “My son wants to be an inventor now!”
A visiting teacher asked, “Where did you learn to do this?” Ishmael grinned. “In the attic!”
In the afternoon, an ambulance drove past the school. A student asked, “Who drives that?”
“Someone who helps sick people,” said Hannah, “like a nurse or a doctor.” “We have to learn about safety too,” added Ishmael, “so that we do not need an ambulance.”
Later, the class packed up. “Put the tools in the folder,” said the teacher, “and then clean up.”
Hannah asked, “Should we wash the spoons in the sink?” “No,” said the teacher, smiling. “Use the new dishwasher the school bought last week!” Everyone laughed — it was their first time using it.
At home that night, Hannah brushed her teeth with her new toothbrush and toothpaste. She looked at her reflection and whispered, “I helped build a flying machine.”
Her mother came in. “Did you learn something today?” she asked.
“Yes,” said Hannah. “I learned that tools help us — but teamwork builds the best things of all.”

grammar.title: Defining Relative Clauses (who, that, which)
grammar.explanation: A defining relative clause is an extra part of a sentence that tells us exactly which person or thing we mean. For example, in “the tool that joins papers,” the words “that joins papers” tell us it is a stapler. This helps us describe the tools and machines in this unit clearly.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
who → for people:  “She is the teacher who helps us.”
which → for things and animals:  “The car which is parked outside is blue.”
that → for people, animals, or things:  “A machine that heats food is a microwave.”
Tip on the pattern: start with a noun, add who / that / which, then add the extra information — “The crew that worked hard finished early.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “The teacher which helps us is nice.”  ✗
Fix: use who for people: “The teacher who helps us is nice.”  ✓
Also remember to include the relative word. Mistake: “The girl runs fast is my friend.” ✗  Fix: “The girl who runs fast is my friend.” ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think: who for people (like a superhero), which for things (like your favourite toy), and that for almost anything.
grammar.practice: Fill in the blank with who, that, or which: | The film ______ was on TV last night was funny. | The girl ______ won the race is my sister. | I have a machine ______ heats food quickly. | The book ______ you lent me was amazing. | The nurse ______ helps sick people is kind.

grammar.title: Using “have to” for Obligation
grammar.explanation: When we say we have to do something, we mean it is necessary — we must do it. This unit uses “have to” for safety rules, like wearing gloves or being careful with sharp tools.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Subject + have to / has to + the base form of the verb.
I / you / we / they → have to:  “We have to wear equipment to stay safe.”
he / she / it → has to:  “She has to finish her homework.”
Questions: “Do you have to go?”   Negative: “I don't have to go.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “She have to clean the attic.” ✗    Fix: “She has to clean the attic.” ✓
Also use the base verb: say “have to go”, not “have to going”.
grammar.memoryTip: Think of “have to” as your must-do list. If it is on the list, you have to do it!
grammar.practice: Complete each sentence with have to or has to and the verb in brackets: | I ______ (finish) my homework before dinner. | She ______ (take) the bus to school every day. | We ______ (wear) helmets when riding bikes. | They ______ (clean) the classroom after school. | ______ he ______ (help) his sister with her project?

grammar.title: Future Tense with “will” for Promises
grammar.explanation: We use will to talk about the future, especially to make a promise. Saying “I will” shows that we mean to keep our word, like promising to keep your folder tidy.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Subject + will + the base form of the verb.
“I will keep my folder tidy.”
“We will build something useful.”
Negative: add not → “I will not forget.”  Short form: “I won't forget.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “I will keeps my room tidy.” ✗    Fix: use the base verb: “I will keep my room tidy.” ✓
Do not forget the subject: say “I will clean my room”, not “Will clean my room”.
grammar.memoryTip: Think of will as a “promise helper”. Whenever you promise something, “will” is right there to help.
grammar.practice: Complete each sentence with the correct form using will: | I ______ (help) you with your project. | She ______ (not forget) to bring snacks. | They ______ (clean) the classroom tomorrow. | We ______ (be) friends forever.

grammar.title: The Past Simple Tense
grammar.explanation: The past simple tells about actions that already happened and are finished. It helps us tell stories, like when the class found old tools in the attic.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Regular verbs: add -ed → play → played, jump → jumped.
Verbs ending in -e: add just -d → like → liked.
Irregular verbs: you must learn these → go → went, find → found, have → had.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “I find my keys yesterday.” ✗    Fix: “I found my keys yesterday.” ✓
Mistake with irregular verbs: “She goed to the store.” ✗    Fix: “She went to the store.” ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of the -ed ending as a “time stamp” that says: this already happened!
grammar.practice: Write the past simple form of the verb in brackets: | I ______ (find) a coin on the ground. | They ______ (play) games all afternoon. | She ______ (go) to the market last week. | We ______ (visit) our friends on Saturday. | He ______ (cook) breakfast this morning.

grammar.title: Personal Pronouns and Tense
grammar.explanation: Personal pronouns — I, you, he, she, it, we, they — take the place of a noun so we do not repeat it. We also change the verb to show past or future. This keeps our stories clear.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Choose the right pronoun for the person or thing (she for a girl, it for an object).
Past: use the past verb → “She baked cookies.”
Future: use will + base verb → “We will travel next summer.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “She is my brother.” ✗    Fix: a brother is a boy → “He is my brother.” ✓
Mistake: mixing tenses → “I will played soccer yesterday.” ✗    Fix: “I played soccer yesterday.” ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Pronouns are the “stars” of your sentence, and tense is “time travel”: back to the past, or forward to the future.
grammar.practice: Fill in the correct pronoun and the right past or future verb: | ______ (I) ______ (go) to the zoo last weekend. | ______ (We) ______ (play) football tomorrow. | ______ (He) ______ (watch) a film last night. | ______ (She) ______ (finish) her homework later today.

grammar.title: Cause and Effect Connectors (because, so that) + Imperatives (Giving Instructions)
grammar.explanation: These connectors explain why something happens or for what purpose. They make our ideas clearer and join two thoughts together.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
because → gives the reason:  “She was late because she missed the bus.”
so that → gives the purpose:  “I study hard so that I can pass my exams.”
You can use so for a more casual purpose: “I wear a jacket so it won't be cold.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: leaving out the connector → “He wore a mask it was dusty.” ✗
Fix: “He wore a mask because it was dusty.” ✓   And do not swap them: use because for a reason, so that for a purpose.
grammar.memoryTip: because answers “Why?”   ·   so that answers “For what purpose?”
grammar.practice: Fill in the blank with because or so that: | I bring my umbrella ______ it might rain. | She went to bed early ______ she could wake up fresh. | He loves to paint ______ it makes him happy. | They wore helmets ______ they would be safe while biking. | Lesson 7:  Imperatives (Giving Instructions) | What it means and why it matters | Imperatives are command words that tell someone what to do. We use them for clear instructions, like “Use your tools safely” or “Put the parts away”. | How to form it | Start with the base verb — no subject needed: “Clean your room.” | Add details: “Carry the books to the library.” | Be polite with “please”: “Please put your shoes away.” | Say “don't” for a negative: “Don't run in the hallway.” | A common mistake | Mistake: adding an “s” → “Uses your utensils properly.” ✗ | Fix: imperatives use the base verb → “Use your utensils properly.” ✓  Do not add a subject either: say “Clean your room”, not “You clean your room”. | Memory tip | Imperative = Verb + what you want them to do. It is like giving a friendly command. | Practice 7 | Write the correct imperative using the verb in brackets: | ______ your hands before eating.  (Wash) | ______ your toys after playing.  (Put away) | ______ your homework every day.  (Complete) | ______ carefully with the sharp hardware.  (Work)

speaking.title: Speaking 1: Describe an Everyday Item
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose an object from your Vocabulary list. Describe it without saying its name, and let someone guess. Use a relative clause:
“It is a machine that heats food quickly.”  (microwave)
“It is a tool that joins papers together.”  (stapler)
“It is a vehicle that takes sick people to hospital.”  (ambulance)
Now describe three objects of your own. Record yourself and listen back.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise describe an everyday item and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 2: Safety Rules
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Tell a partner or your AI tutor three safety rules, using have to:
“We have to wear gloves when we clean the attic.”
“You have to be careful with sharp hardware.”
“We have to ______.”  (your own idea)
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise safety rules and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 3: What Am I?
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
One person thinks of a tool, machine, or everyday item.
The others ask questions: “Is it a machine that ______?” or “Do you use it in the kitchen?”
Keep asking until someone guesses the object. Then swap turns.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise what am i? and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 4: Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six unit words. Ask and answer a clear question about each word using complete sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise vocabulary expert interview and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 5: Explain and Respond
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Explain one important unit idea, give a reason with because, and respond to two follow-up questions.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise explain and respond and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 6: Three-Minute Unit Talk
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Plan and record a three-minute talk using an opening, three connected ideas with evidence or examples, and a purposeful closing.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise three-minute unit talk and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

writing.title: Writing 1: An Object Description Poster
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Choose a useful tool or item (for example a stapler, a shield, or a microwave). Draw it, then write three sentences about it. Use at least one adjective and one relative clause:
“This is my microwave. It is a white machine that heats food quickly. I use it to warm my soup.”
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about an object description poster.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 2: Instructions: How to Use It Safely
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write four steps to explain how to use one tool or machine safely. Use imperatives and have to:
Title: “How to Use a ______ Safely”
Step 1: ______.   Step 2: ______.   Step 3: ______.   Step 4: ______.
Include one sentence with “You have to ______ so that ______.”
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about instructions: how to use it safely.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 3: A Future Promise
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write two promises about looking after your things, using will:
“I will keep my folder tidy.”
“We will ______.”
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about a future promise.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 4: Connected Paragraph
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a six-sentence paragraph about the unit. Begin with a topic sentence and join ideas with and, but or because.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about connected paragraph.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 5: Information Text
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a heading and five accurate facts. Use precise unit vocabulary and check spelling and punctuation.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about information text.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 6: Reflect and Improve
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Choose earlier writing, revise three sentences, add two useful details, and explain one improvement you made.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about reflect and improve.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work

activities.title: Activities 1: Match the Tool to the Job
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Match each item to what it does. Write the letter next to the number.
stapler
microwave
telescope
ambulance
a) heats food quickly   b) takes sick people to hospital   c) joins papers together   d) helps you see the stars
Answers: 1. c   2. a   3. d   4. b
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 2: Choose the Right Word
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Fill each blank with a word from the unit.
Flour is an ______ for making bread.  (ingredient / telescope)
The knight held a ______ to protect himself.  (shield / folder)
We found old toys in the ______ at the top of the house.  (crew / attic)
The factory has big ______ that helps make cars.  (machinery / cocoa)
Answers: 1. ingredient   2. shield   3. attic   4. machinery
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 3: Finish the Relative Clause
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Complete each sentence with “who”, “that”, or “which”.
A microwave is a machine ______ heats food quickly.
A crew is a group of people ______ work together.
This is the briefcase ______ my dad takes to work.
Answers: 1. that (or which)   2. who   3. that (or which)
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 4: Critical Thinking
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Talk or write about these questions. There is no single right answer — explain your reasons with because and so that.
Which item is the most important in your home? Why?
What would happen if we had no tools or machines?
Why does a crew often work better than one person alone?
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 5: Vocabulary Sort and Explain
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Sort twelve unit words into useful categories. Explain the rule for each category in a complete sentence.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 6: Reading Evidence Hunt
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Find six important details in the unit texts. Record the detail and explain what it helps the reader understand.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

assignments.title: Tools, Machines, and Everyday Items learning portfolio
assignments.instructions: Submit Writing 1: An Object Description Poster and a recording of Speaking 1: Describe an Everyday Item. Improve both pieces using the Grade 4 rubrics.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.quizTitle: Tools, Machines, and Everyday Items checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'equipment' mean?
quizzes.options: A flat object that protects you from something harmful. | A strong bag used to carry papers and important work things. | A tool that holds pieces of paper together with a small metal clip. | Things that are used for a special job or task, like tools or machines.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Things that are used for a special job or task, like tools or machines.
quizzes.explanation: We need special equipment to play football, like a ball and goalposts.

quizzes.quizTitle: Tools, Machines, and Everyday Items checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'folder' mean?
quizzes.options: A cover or container used to hold papers and keep them tidy. | A flat object that protects you from something harmful. | A tool that holds pieces of paper together with a small metal clip. | The solid tools, parts, or machines you can touch and use to build or fix things.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A cover or container used to hold papers and keep them tidy.
quizzes.explanation: I put my homework in my folder so it does not get lost.

quizzes.quizTitle: Tools, Machines, and Everyday Items checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'briefcase' mean?
quizzes.options: A strong bag used to carry papers and important work things. | A tool that holds pieces of paper together with a small metal clip. | Big machines that work together to do a heavy job. | The solid tools, parts, or machines you can touch and use to build or fix things.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A strong bag used to carry papers and important work things.
quizzes.explanation: My dad takes his briefcase to the office every day.

quizzes.quizTitle: Tools, Machines, and Everyday Items checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'shield' mean?
quizzes.options: A flat object that protects you from something harmful. | Big machines that work together to do a heavy job. | The solid tools, parts, or machines you can touch and use to build or fix things. | Useful materials or tools for work or learning, like books, tools, or money.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A flat object that protects you from something harmful.
quizzes.explanation: The knight held a shield to protect himself from arrows.

quizzes.quizTitle: Tools, Machines, and Everyday Items checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'stapler' mean?
quizzes.options: A piece of cloth that hangs in front of a window or on a stage. | A tool that holds pieces of paper together with a small metal clip. | Big machines that work together to do a heavy job. | Useful materials or tools for work or learning, like books, tools, or money.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A tool that holds pieces of paper together with a small metal clip.
quizzes.explanation: I used a stapler to keep my homework papers together.

quizzes.quizTitle: Tools, Machines, and Everyday Items checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'hardware' mean?
quizzes.options: A piece of cloth that hangs in front of a window or on a stage. | Something you use to make food or a dish. | The solid tools, parts, or machines you can touch and use to build or fix things. | Useful materials or tools for work or learning, like books, tools, or money.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The solid tools, parts, or machines you can touch and use to build or fix things.
quizzes.explanation: We bought some hardware to fix the broken chair.

quizzes.quizTitle: Tools, Machines, and Everyday Items checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'machinery' mean?
quizzes.options: A brown powder made from cacao beans, used to make chocolate drinks and treats. | A piece of cloth that hangs in front of a window or on a stage. | Big machines that work together to do a heavy job. | Something you use to make food or a dish.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Big machines that work together to do a heavy job.
quizzes.explanation: The factory has a lot of big machinery that helps make cars.

quizzes.quizTitle: Tools, Machines, and Everyday Items checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'resources' mean?
quizzes.options: A brown powder made from cacao beans, used to make chocolate drinks and treats. | A machine that cooks or heats food quickly. | Something you use to make food or a dish. | Useful materials or tools for work or learning, like books, tools, or money.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Useful materials or tools for work or learning, like books, tools, or money.
quizzes.explanation: We need resources like pencils and paper for our art project.

quizzes.quizTitle: Tools, Machines, and Everyday Items checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'curtain' mean?
quizzes.options: A brown powder made from cacao beans, used to make chocolate drinks and treats. | A machine that cooks or heats food quickly. | A piece of cloth that hangs in front of a window or on a stage. | A tool used for eating or cooking, like a fork or a spoon.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A piece of cloth that hangs in front of a window or on a stage.
quizzes.explanation: She pulled the curtain open to let in the sunlight.

quizzes.quizTitle: Tools, Machines, and Everyday Items checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'ingredient' mean?
quizzes.options: A machine that cooks or heats food quickly. | A small tool with a handle and bristles for cleaning your teeth. | A tool used for eating or cooking, like a fork or a spoon. | Something you use to make food or a dish.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Something you use to make food or a dish.
quizzes.explanation: Flour is an important ingredient for making bread.

teacherNotes.note: Model new language aloud, check meaning before independent work, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and vocabulary precision.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you read the microwave talk with me and help me list the steps?”
“Can you play a listening game about tools and machines?”
“Can you explain any words I did not understand in the reading?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me describe an object using ‘that’ or ‘which’?”
“Can you play ‘What Am I?’ with me?”
“Can you check that my safety sentences with ‘have to’ are correct?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my object poster and help me add a relative clause?”
“Are my safety instructions clear and in the right order?”
“Can you help me make my promises sound polite and clear?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you quiz me on the tool and equipment words?”
“What is the difference between hardware and machinery?”
“Can you help me describe a tool using ‘that’ or ‘which’?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you quiz me on the household and kitchen words?”
“Can you help me write a safe way to use the microwave?”
“What ingredients do I need to make a simple snack?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you quiz me on the transport and work words?”
“Why does a crew work better than one person alone?”
“Can you help me make a sentence about a telescope?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my grammar answers?”
“Why do we say ‘she has to’ but ‘we have to’?”
“Can you give me five more sentences to practise relative clauses?”

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.
answerKey.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Things that are used for a special job or task, like tools or machines. We need special equipment to play football, like a ball and goalposts.
A cover or container used to hold papers and keep them tidy. I put my homework in my folder so it does not get lost.
A strong bag used to carry papers and important work things. My dad takes his briefcase to the office every day.
A flat object that protects you from something harmful. The knight held a shield to protect himself from arrows.
A tool that holds pieces of paper together with a small metal clip. I used a stapler to keep my homework papers together.
The solid tools, parts, or machines you can touch and use to build or fix things. We bought some hardware to fix the broken chair.
Big machines that work together to do a heavy job. The factory has a lot of big machinery that helps make cars.
Useful materials or tools for work or learning, like books, tools, or money. We need resources like pencils and paper for our art project.
A piece of cloth that hangs in front of a window or on a stage. She pulled the curtain open to let in the sunlight.
Something you use to make food or a dish. Flour is an important ingredient for making bread.
